revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23984597483|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[ja:冨士御室浅間神社]] {{distinguish|{{ill|Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja|大室山浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmuroyama Sengen Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}|{{ill|Hokutō-hongū Omuro Sengen Shrine|ja|小室浅間神社_(富士吉田市大明見)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokutō-hongū Omuro Sengen Shrine|qq=}}|{{ill|Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine|qq=}}}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine |image=
main hall |location=Okumiya (former main shrine)
{{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|qid=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}} {{ill|Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi|en|Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi|qid=Q1141178|lt=Minamitsuru District|1=Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi}} {{ill|Fujikawaguchiko|en|Fujikawaguchiko|qid=Q1004231|lt=Fujikawaguchiko|1=Fujikawaguchiko}} Katsuyama (Second station of Mount Fuji, detached area)
Satomiya (current main shrine)
Yamanashi Prefecture, Minamitsuru District, Fujikawaguchiko, Katsuyama 3951 |coordinates=Okumiya (former main shrine)
{{coord|35|24|0.03|N|138|45|18.60|E|region:JP-19_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine}}
Satomiya (current main shrine)
{{coord|35|30|38.15|N|138|44|45.58|E|region:JP-19_type:landmark|name=Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine Satomiya (current main shrine)}} |deity={{ill|Konohanasakuya-hime|ja|木花咲耶姫命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohanasakuya-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ}} |shintai= |shakaku=Former {{ill|Prefectural Shrine|ja|近代Rank制度#諸社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|qid=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} |established=3rd year of Emperor Monmu (699) |honden= |other_names=Komuro Sengen Myōjin |fudasyo_etc= |main_festival=April 25, September 9 |shinji= |map={{OSM location map | coord={{coord|35|24|0.03|N|138|45|18.60|E}} | zoom=10 | float=center | width=260 | height=240 | caption= | title= | minimap=file | mini-file=map of Yamanashi Prefecture Ja.svg | mini-width=100 | mini-height=100 | minipog-x=54 | minipog-y=67 | scalemark=60 | shape1=image | mark1=Red pog.svg | label1=奥宮（旧本宮） | mark-size1=6 | mark-coord1={{coord|35|24|0.03|N|138|45|18.60|E}} | label-pos1=top | label-size1=12 |label-color1=black |mark-description1=冨士御室浅間神社奥宮（旧本宮） | mark2=Red pog.svg | label2=里宮・現本宮 | mark-size2=6 | mark-coord2={{coord|35|30|38.15|N|138|44|45.58|E}} | label-pos2=bottom | label-size2=12 |label-color2=black |mark-description2=冨士御室浅間神社里宮・現本宮 }} }} '''Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine''' (冨士御室浅間神社, Fuji Omuro Sengen Jinja) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Fujikawaguchiko|en|Fujikawaguchiko|qid=Q1004231|lt=Fujikawaguchiko|1=Fujikawaguchiko}}, {{ill|Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi|en|Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi|qid=Q1141178|lt=Minamitsuru District|1=Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi}}, {{ill|Yamanashi Prefecture|en|Yamanashi Prefecture|qid=Q132720|lt=Yamanashi Prefecture|1=Yamanashi Prefecture}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代Rank制度|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was that of a Prefectural Shrine, and it is now designated as a Beppyo Shrine under the jurisdiction of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|en|Association of Shinto Shrines|qid=Q908077|lt=Association of Shinto Shrines|1=Association of Shinto Shrines}}. It is one of the many {{ill|Sengen shrine|en|Sengen shrine|qid=Q3471785|lt=Sengen shrines|1=Sengen shrine}} across Japan. The shrine was formerly known as Komuro Sengen Myōjin and is considered the oldest shrine on Mount Fuji. The shrine is registered as a part of the World Cultural Heritage site "Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration." ==Overview== The shrine consists of the '''Okumiya''' (former main shrine) and the '''Satomiya''' (current main shrine). The Okumiya, located at the former main shrine site, is situated at the second station of the Yoshida Trail on Mount Fuji. The grounds of this Okumiya lie within a detached area of {{ill|Fujikawaguchiko|en|Fujikawaguchiko|qid=Q1004231|lt=Fujikawaguchiko|1=Fujikawaguchiko}}, within {{ill|Fujiyoshida|en|Fujiyoshida|qid=Q647508|lt=Fujiyoshida|1=Fujiyoshida}}. It is considered the first shrine enshrined in the mountains of Mount Fuji. The Satomiya is situated in Katsuyama, near {{ill|Lake Kawaguchi|en|Lake Kawaguchi|qid=Q577372|lt=Lake Kawaguchi|1=Lake Kawaguchi}}. The main shrine was relocated to an area adjacent to the Satomiya in 1973, replacing the former Okumiya. A new Okumiya was then established at the original site. File:Fujiomurosengen-satomiya-honden.jpg|main hall at the Satomiya, single structure with curved gable roof, built in Meiji 22 (1889) Next to the Satomiya is the {{ill|Katsuyama Folklore Museum|ja|勝山民俗資料館|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuyama Folklore Museum|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which exhibits the history of Katsuyama. ==History== The shrine is said to have been founded in the 3rd year of Emperor Monmu (699) by {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoshitada|ja|藤原義忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Yoshitada|1=Fujiwara no Yoshitada|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17212403}}. The name "Omuro" (御室) is derived from the practice of conducting rituals within a circle of stone pillars. In the 2nd year of Tentoku (958), Emperor {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} established a Satomiya on the south bank of Lake Kawaguchi for the convenience of the parishioners. The shrine developed through associations with {{ill|Shugendō|en|Shugendō|qid=Q1483875|lt=Shugendō|1=Shugendō}} during the medieval period and with the {{ill|Fujiko (religion)|en|Fujiko (religion)|qid=Q11456201|lt=Fuji cult|1=Fujiko (religion)}} during the early modern period. It was particularly revered by the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|qid=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}} during the Sengoku period. The current shrine buildings were reconstructed in 1889. The current main hall was constructed in Keichō 17 (1612) by {{ill|Torii Naritsugu|en|Torii Naritsugu|qid=Q3336101|lt=Torii Naritsugu|1=Torii Naritsugu}}, a retainer of the Tokugawa family. The structure has undergone four major renovations, and in 1973 it was relocated from the second station of Mount Fuji to a site adjacent to the Satomiya. The building is a single structure with a gabled roof and curved gable at the entrance, featuring characteristics of the Momoyama period, and is designated as a National Important Cultural Property. During the Meiji period, the shrine was renamed from "Komuro Sengen Shrine" to "Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine." Meanwhile, the "Fuji San Shitamiya Komuro Sengen Shrine" in Shimoyoshida, Fujiyoshida, and the "Asodani Miyamori Shrine" in {{ill|Ōasomi|ja|大明見|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōasomi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Fujiyoshida, were renamed to "Komuro Sengen Shrine." The shrine was Ranked as a Prefectural Shrine in Taishō 14 (1925) and became a Beppyo Shrine in Shōwa 46 (1971). The shrine's head priest, who held the title "Kosano Echigo no Kami" (越後守), was granted various privileges and exemptions by the {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|qid=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}} during the Sengoku period, including the administration of other Sengen shrines. ==festivals== ; Yabusame (April 29) : The festival originated in Tenkei 3 (940) when {{ill|Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato|2=ja|3=藤原秀郷|ja|Fujiwara no Hidesato|1=Fujiwara no Hidesato|2=ja|3=藤原秀郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Hidesato|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1136623}} offered a thanksgiving ceremony and yabusame (archery on horseback) to celebrate his victory over {{ill|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|ja|Taira no Masakado|1=Taira no Masakado|2=en|3=Taira no Masakado|4=ja|5=平将門|6=de|7=Taira no Masakado|8=es|9=Taira no Masakado|10=fr|11=Taira no Masakado|lt=Taira no Masakado|qid=Q1207652|1=Taira no Masakado|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Masakado|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. : Previously, the yabusame event was held at Ryūgakebaba near Chūnō Chaya on the Yoshida Trail and included multiple villages, including Shimoyoshida (now the site of Fuji San Shitamiya Komuro Sengen Shrine). However, disputes over the leadership of the event led to a decree by {{ill|Takeda clan|en|Takeda clan|qid=Q1210114|lt=Takeda clan|1=Takeda clan}} retainer {{ill|Itagaki Nobukata|en|Itagaki Nobukata|qid=Q1359972|lt=Itagaki Nobukata|1=Itagaki Nobukata}} in Kyōroku 3 (1530), which allowed each village to hold its own event. : The yabusame festival was interrupted in Meiji 8 (1875), resumed and halted several times, and ceased in Meiji 32 (1899). The main festival was then replaced by the Shibasai festival on September 9, centered around a mikoshi (portable shrine) procession{{Cite book|title=吉田口登山道の騮ヶ馬場と流鏑馬|date=2019.1.1|year=2019.1.1|publisher=山梨県立富士山世界遺産センター}}. : Since Shōwa 55 (1980), the yabusame has been revived by inviting Takeda-style yabusame from {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|qid=Q701403|lt=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|1=Tsurugaoka Hachimangū}} in Kamakura and held at Shikkogo Park. ==Cultural Properties== === Important Cultural Properties (National Designation) === ; main hall : Relocated and reconstructed in 1973 from the second station of Mount Fuji, completed in May of the following year. It is a gabled structure with copper roofing. According to the cultural record 'Kai Kokushi' from Bunka 11 (1814), it was originally built in Keichō 17 (1612). === Yamanashi Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Properties === ; Katsuyama-ki : A chronicle transcribed by monks of {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|ja|Nichiren宗|1=Nichiren-shū|2=ja|3=日蓮宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nichiren Buddhism|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q206611}}'s Jōren-ji in the Lake Kawaguchi area from Bunshō 1 (1466) to Eiroku 6 (1563). The text, known as 'Katsuyama-ki' or 'Myōhō-ji-ki,' is considered the closest to the original text among the various versions. It was originally untitled, later named during the Edo period's 'Kai Kokushi' compilation but not reflected at that time, renamed "Katsuyama Nikki" by Tanaka Yoshinari in Meiji 22 (1889), and recently given the title "Katsuyama-ki" again. Designated on March 17, 1975. ; Anzan Kiganjō : A petition document dedicated by [[Takeda Shingen]]{{efn|Japanese feudal lord (1521–1573)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeda Shingen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Harunobu) on November 19, Kōji 3 (1557), praying for the safe delivery and health of his eldest daughter, {{ill|Hōjō Ujimasa|en|Hōjō Ujimasa|qid=Q736948|lt=Hōjō Ujizane|1=Hōjō Ujimasa}}'s wife, {{ill|Kōbai-in|ja|黄梅院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbai-in|1=Ōbai-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11677778}}. The document promised to remove the checkpoint at Funatsu (Fujikawaguchiko) on the Kamakura highway if the prayer was fulfilled. Included in the "Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine Documents." 'Sengoku Ibun' Takeda clan edition - 579th issue. : Shingen also dedicated similar petitions in Eiroku 9 (1566), promising to lift the Kurokoma checkpoint on the Kamakura highway. These petitions, accompanied by the Great Perfection Sutra reading led by his vassal Ichikawa Iemitsu and the priest Kosano Echigo no Kami, were intended to strengthen alliances with the Hōjō clan and improve the convenience of the Kamakura highway, a route of Fuji worship. === Historic Site === * Included in the National Historic Site "Mount Fuji." ==location== ; Okumiya (former main shrine): Katsuyama, Fujikawaguchiko, Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi Prefecture (Second station of Mount Fuji) ; Satomiya (current main shrine): Katsuyama 3951, Fujikawaguchiko, Minamitsuru District, Yamanashi Prefecture ==Footnotes== {{Reflist}} ==References== * Hirohira Nishikawa, "On the Offering of Petitions by Takeda Shingen - An Aspect of Religious Policy," in The New Complete Works of Takeda Shingen. ==Related Articles== * {{ill|Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|ja|北口本宮冨士浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitaguchi Hongū Fuji Sengen Shrine|qq=}} - A shrine located in Kamiyoshida. According to 'Kai Kokushi,' the Sengen deity was transferred from the second station shrine to here, with the second station shrine being called "Upper Sengen" and the Kitaguchi shrine "Lower Sengen." * {{ill|Omuro Sengen Shrine (Yamanashi)|lt=Omuro Sengen Shrine (Yamanashi)|lt=Fuji San Shitamiya Komuro Sengen Shrine|ja|冨士山下宮小室浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji San Shitamiya Komuro Sengen Shrine|qq=}} - Located in Shimoyoshida. The shrine and its rites, including the yabusame event, were originally conducted together with the second station shrine. * {{ill|Hokutō-hongū Omuro Sengen Shrine|lt=Hokutō-hongū Omuro Sengen Shrine|lt=Komuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|ja|小室浅間神社 (富士吉田市大明見)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komuro Sengen Shrine (Ōasomi, Fujiyoshida)|qq=}} - Renamed to "Komuro Sengen Shrine" during the Meiji period, along with Fuji San Shitamiya Komuro Sengen Shrine. * {{ill|Rengezan Myōhō-ji|ja|蓮華山妙法寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rengezan Myōhō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Katsuyama-ki|ja|勝山記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsuyama-ki|1=Katsuyama-ki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11400263}} * {{ill|Kōshū Dōguyū|ja|郡内大工仲間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshū Dōguyū|qq=}} ==External Links== * [http://fujiomurosengenjinja.jp/index.html Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine] (official site) * [http://www.katuyama.info/kanko/sengen.html Fuji Omuro Sengen Shrine] (Katsuyama Tourism Association & Katsuyama Minshuku Association website) {{Historic Sites Mount Fuji}} {{Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} [[File:Fuji-omuro-sengen-jinja-Hongu.jpg|285px|main hall (National Important Cultural Property)]] {{translated page|ja|冨士御室浅間神社|version=99046130|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:冨士御室浅間神社]] [[ja:Fuji Omuro Asama Shrine]] {{Commons category|Fuji-Omuro-sengen-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q11395081}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fujikawaguchiko, Yamanashi]] [[Category:Cultural heritage monuments in Japan]] [[Category:Edo period architecture]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Fujisan, sacred place and source of artistic inspiration]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:History of Fujikawaguchiko Town]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties (buildings)]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Property of Japan in Yamanashi prefecture]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:National historic sites in Yamanashi prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Yamanashi Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
